steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage ends with "This suggests that ______" — it needs a logical inference from the evidence shown. The key evidence: Darwin's fall 1838 notebook labels the struggle-for-survival idea as "inference from Malthus," citing an 1798 economics essay, then his October 1838 notebook uses strikingly similar language ("grand crush of population"). The inference must explain what that sequence suggests.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — though some of Darwin's notebook entries relating to his theory of natural selection date from 1838, others must have been written as late as 1858.: states a date-range fact but draws no inference from the Malthus connection. ✗
  • Choice B — in early fall 1838, Darwin realized that Malthus's ideas regarding human populations derived from observations of animal behavior in nature.: reverses the direction of influence — Malthus influenced Darwin, not the other way around. ✗
  • Choice C — sometime in or before October 1838, Darwin determined that a postulate rooted in economics might also be applicable to biology.: directly matches the passage's evidence — Darwin borrowed an economic concept (Malthus on population outpacing production) and applied it to biological systems. ✓
  • Choice D — a key concept in Darwin's theory of natural selection had been previously articulated in the same terms by economists sometime between 1798 and 1838.: the passage shows Darwin adopted a Malthusian premise, not that economists had already stated natural selection. ✗
That is the correct answer.
